main_summary.summary: Joint interview with Joseph (Joe) Howell and Embry Howell, conducted December 13, 2015 by David Cline of the Southern Oral History Program for the Civil Rights History Project. Joe Howell was born 1942 in Nashville, Tennessee, son of a Nashville City Banks president, raised in the Belle Meade neighborhood; he contracted polio in 1952 and spent six months at Warm Springs, an experience he credits with developing empathy. Embry Howell was born 1945 in Bethesda, Maryland and raised in Davidson, North Carolina, where her father D. Grier Martin served as treasurer and later president of Davidson College and worked behind the scenes to integrate the institution. As a Davidson College senior in spring 1964, Joe attended the Third Great American Revolution conference at Bryn Mawr and Haverford, then organized the March on Charlotte, a signature-collection drive supporting the Civil Rights Act of 1964, conducted with Barber-Scotia and Johnson C. Smith students. The Howells later attended Union Theological Seminary, where Charles Sherrod recruited them for SNCC work in Southwest Georgia in summer 1966. They arrived in Albany during the John Lewis–Stokely Carmichael split over the role of white organizers. Allard Lowenstein became a close associate; Dennis Sweeney, Lowenstein's later assassin, was a Union classmate's roommate.
key_themes: Southern white students entering the civil rights movement
Charles Sherrod and SNCC Southwest Georgia Project
Black Power transition and white-organizer tensions
main_summary.historical_significance:
The Howells' joint testimony documents the recruitment pipeline that brought white Southern students from Davidson College and Union Theological Seminary into SNCC's Southwest Georgia project during the pivotal 1966 transition to Black Power. Their account of arriving in Albany to be greeted with 'go home, whitey, we don't need you' captures the John Lewis–Stokely Carmichael split as it was experienced by white workers on the ground, while Embry's observation that black SNCC organizers nonetheless treated her with personal civility complicates the binary public narrative.

Joe's March on Charlotte in spring 1964, a signature-collection campaign in support of the Civil Rights Act conducted jointly with students from Barber-Scotia and Johnson C. Smith, preserves a less-documented Southern white college mobilization moment between the March on Washington and Freedom Summer. The interview also documents the Allard Lowenstein recruitment network and the Dennis Sweeney connection through Union Theological Seminary, linking the oral history to a later tragic chapter of movement aftermath.

chapters.title: A College President's Daughter
chapters.summary: Interviewed by David Cline for the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian NMAAHC on December 13, 2015, Joseph and Embry Howell introduce themselves, and Embry tells of being born in 1945 in Bethesda, Maryland, raised first in Bristol, Virginia, then on the campus of Davidson College in North Carolina, where her father was treasurer and ultimately president.

chapters.title: Across the Tracks in Davidson
chapters.summary: Embry describes parents born and raised in Georgia and unusually well-educated grandparents, a mother who attended communist meetings in 1930s New York, a father whose presidency helped integrate Davidson with its first students from Africa around 1963, and her own segregated schooling alongside friends she crossed the tracks to play with, learning that all people have dignity.

chapters.title: Belle Meade, Banking, and Polio
chapters.summary: Joe recounts being born in 1942 in Nashville and raised in the elite Belle Meade neighborhood, the son and grandson of bank presidents, expected to become a banker and sent to Montgomery Bell Academy, and contracting polio in 1952 and convalescing at Warm Springs, an experience that left him moderately paralyzed, cost him two years of school, and gave him a lasting empathy for people who were suffering.

chapters.title: The Controversial Student
chapters.summary: Joe tells of arriving at Davidson, where he met the much-younger Embry and became a campus leader as president of the YMCA and ODK, while growing aware of the early sit-in movement planned in Nashville, and recalls the childhood playmate Frederick, the nephew of his family's housekeeper, whom he was told he could no longer play with, an injustice he found a no-brainer even as a boy.

chapters.title: You Can't Be Neutral
chapters.summary: Joe describes a 1964 conference at Bryn Mawr called the Third Great American Revolution, where lieutenants of CORE, SNCC, and SCLC urged students that they could not be neutral, and how, recalling the Freedom Riders who had passed through Davidson, he and a handful of students drove home and resolved to mount a march on Charlotte gathering signatures for the Civil Rights Act of 1964 to mail to President Johnson, partnering with schools like Barber-Scotia and Johnson C. Smith.

chapters.title: A Hint of a Wink
chapters.summary: Joe recounts being summoned at night to the home of Davidson's president, D. Grier Martin, who, under pressure from the Board of Trustees and the mayor of Charlotte, asked him to abandon the march while stressing he could not force him, all with a hint of a wink that signaled private support, the president working behind the scenes to advance integration at Davidson.

chapters.title: Banker's Son Leads Rights March
chapters.summary: Joe tells how the march drew wire-service coverage and a conservative Nashville headline reading Banker's Son Leads Rights March, naming him as the son of a city bank president, how his parents proved supportive amid the elite cocktail-party set, and how he became a pariah, turned away at a friend's door as a discredit to his race, a woman he came to pity as a product of her time.

chapters.title: A Pied Piper Named Al
chapters.summary: Joe describes his friendship with Allard Lowenstein, who taught at North Carolina State and sent him notes scribbled on the backs of tests by special delivery, a former National Student Association president and Yale Law graduate close to Eleanor Roosevelt and the Kennedy family, a charismatic pied piper of the dump-Johnson movement later assassinated by a mentally ill former student.

chapters.part: Seminary, Marriage, and a Call to Georgia
chapters.title: Into the Big Leagues
chapters.summary: Joe recounts a Rockefeller Fellowship taking him to Union Theological Seminary in New York, where Paul Tillich and Reinhold Niebuhr had taught, a year out working in an urban service-training program, and a path through city planning, and Embry tells her side of their courtship, marrying after her sophomore year, transferring to Barnard while they lived at Hastings Hall, then both heading to graduate school in Chapel Hill.

chapters.part: Seminary, Marriage, and a Call to Georgia
chapters.title: I Need You Down in Southwest Georgia
chapters.summary: The Howells describe being recruited at Union Theological Seminary by Charles Sherrod, a charismatic SNCC founder working in Southwest Georgia who looked them in the eyes and asked for their help, and recall a pre-summer training conference near Wake Forest, a radical Christian ecumenical institute they found to be thought control, where civil rights workers advised them to back their car in for a quick getaway.

chapters.title: Go Home, Whitey
chapters.summary: Arriving in Southwest Georgia after driving all night to the SNCC office in the Albany neighborhood called Harlem, the Howells were met by workers who told them panthers eat roosters and to go home, an unsettling welcome they came to understand through the 1966 struggle between John Lewis and Stokely Carmichael over whether white Southerners still had a role, the summer Carmichael and Willie Ricks raised the call that confused where the couple could fit.

chapters.title: I Couldn't See the Black Doctor
chapters.summary: Embry reflects on being treated with kid gloves as a white Southern woman whose Georgia upbringing let her relate easily to local Black people, while the color line still cut hard: a white woman who refused her a ride, a serious infection that forced her to a white doctor rather than the Black one, and a reluctant Black barbershop that gave Joe a haircut, all leading to the central question of where a married couple could live.

loc_video.caption: Joseph Howell and Embry Howell oral history interview conducted by David Cline in Washington, District of Columbia, 2015 December 13
